note: Blau, Bonilla, Chades, Dezfouli, 'Optimizing Sequential Experimental Design with Deep Reinforcement Learning', ICML 2022 (PMLR 162; arXiv:2202.00821v3). The paper formulates sequential Bayesian optimal experimental design (BOED) as a Hidden-Parameter MDP and learns the design policy with deep reinforcement learning (REDQ). The RL expected return is constructed to provably equal the SPCE lower bound on the total expected information gain (EIG): Theorems 1-2 give J(pi) = sPCE(pi,L,T) for gamma=1. Thus RL is used to optimize/approximate the EIG-optimal design policy in BOED -- exactly the 'RL for EIG/BOED' the manuscript's L85 clause asserts. Claim C044 is the 'RL~\citep{blau2022rl}' item in a three-way list (variational / amortized-policy / RL) of EIG-approximation approaches; it maps faithfully onto the paper's central result. Bib entry (references.bib L306-312) verified correct against the PDF: title, authors (Blau/Bonilla/Chades/Dezfouli), ICML 2022, arXiv:2202.00821 all match.

C044.evidence: Direct support. Manuscript L85 (CHAR, Sec. after Eq. eq:eid): 'EIG can be approximated via variational methods~\citep{foster2019variational}, amortized policies~\citep{foster2021dad}, and RL~\citep{blau2022rl}.' The blau2022rl half is faithful. Paper Abstract (p.1): 'the problem of optimizing policies can be reduced to solving a Markov decision process (MDP). We solve the equivalent MDP with modern deep reinforcement learning techniques.' Intro (Sec. 1): BOED 'casts this problem as optimization of expected information gain (EIG)'; '...we propose to formulate the sequential experimental design (SED) problem as a Markov decision process (MDP)...and learn the design policy using reinforcement learning (RL). Since our RL agents exploit the Policy Gradient Theorem...there is no need for the EIG objective to be differentiable.' The RL return is tied to the EIG bound explicitly: Theorem 2 (Eq. 16) and Theorem 1 (Eq. 11): 'If gamma = 1 then the expected return satisfies: J(pi) = sPCE(pi, L, T)', where sPCE is the sequential prior contrastive estimation LOWER BOUND on the total EIG (Sec. 2.1, Eq. 6). REDQ (Chen et al. 2021) trains all policies (Sec. 4, App. C.1). So RL is used to optimize a design policy whose objective is a lower-bound approximation of the EIG -- 'EIG can be approximated via ... RL' is an accurate, understated paraphrase, exactly parallel to the sibling variational (foster2019variational) and amortized-policy (foster2021dad/DAD) cites in the same sentence.

reverse_findings: Altitude nuance (not a defect): blau2022rl's RL contribution is optimizing/maximizing the EIG-optimal design POLICY, where the RL return equals the SPCE lower bound on the EIG (Thms 1-2). It does NOT introduce a new EIG *estimator* -- it reuses SPCE / SNMC bounds from Foster et al. (2019/2020/2021). The manuscript verb 'approximated' reads naturally as 'the EIG-optimization problem is tackled via RL' (correct) rather than 'a new numerical EIG estimator is RL-based' (which would be inaccurate). Because the sentence lists RL alongside DAD and variational methods as approaches to the same EIG-maximization problem, the altitude is consistent and the clause is ACCURATE as written; no change needed.

The ordering in the manuscript sentence is well-founded: blau2022rl is the strict generalization of the immediately-preceding cite foster2021dad (DAD, 'amortized policies'). blau2022rl builds on DAD's SPCE bound and permutation-invariant encoder, and extends it to discrete design spaces and non-differentiable / black-box likelihoods where DAD is inapplicable (paper Sec. 3.3, 6; 'a limitation explicitly acknowledged by Foster et al. 2021'). So RL as the third, most general item after variational and amortized-policy methods is a defensible progression, not redundancy.

Bib metadata check (references.bib L306-312): entry is correct -- title 'Optimizing sequential experimental design with deep reinforcement learning', authors Blau/Bonilla/Chades/Dezfouli, booktitle ICML, year 2022, note arXiv:2202.00821. All match the PDF (ICML 2022, PMLR 162, arXiv:2202.00821v3). No metadata error. Minor optional polish only: 'V' in 'Edwin V Bonilla' lacks a period and volume/PMLR 162 is omitted, but neither affects citation correctness.
